Defendants City And County Of San Francisco, Tania Fokin'S Motion For Summary Judgment Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication (Tentative Ruling Purpose Only, Part 2 Of 2)
Off Calendar Jan-25-2022 Off Calendar

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Jan-25-2022 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Shannon Seibert, Esq. (510.679.1981; seibert@sbm.law) of Seibert Bautista Montoya for Plaintiff.
No other appearances.

Not argued. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANTS CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO and TANIA FOKIN'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication

Defendant's motion for summary judgment or alternatively, summary adjudication is denied in its entirety.

Defendant fails to show that the discrimination and retaliation claims fail because plaintiff cannot demonstrate an adverse employment action, i.e., an action that materially affected the terms and conditions of her employment. (See Yanowitz v. L'Oreal USA Inc. (2005) 36 Cal.4th 1028, 1051-1052.) The adduced facts fail to show that plaintiff cannot establish a constructive termination. In addition, there is a triable issue of material fact whether the 2018-2019 performance review constitutes an adverse employment action and whether it was justified. (See Yanowitz v. L'Oreal USA, Inc. (2005) 36 Cal.4th 1028, 1061 [undeserved performance ratings can constitute an adverse employment action]; Horsford v. Board of Trustees of California State University (2005) 132 Cal.App.4th 359, 374 [negative performance reviews constitute adverse employment action]; Cervantes Decl., par. 46.)

Defendant's pretext argument makes little sense since it contends that no adverse employment action occurred. In an event, based on the above, there is a dispute of fact whether defendant's negative performance review was deserved.

Defendant fails to maintain its burden of production on the harassment claims. The proffered facts (UMF 18-34) list numerous instances of inappropriate comments regarding plaintiff's race and disability. Defendant fails to show as a matter of law that the misconduct was not "sufficiently severe or pervasive to alter the conditions of the victim's employment." (Etter v. Veriflo Corp. (1998) 67 Cal.App.4th 457, 463.)

Defendant fails to shift the burden on the retaliation claim. As noted, there is a dispute of fact regarding the existence of an adverse employment action. Defendant also fails to show that there was no nexus between plaintiff's protected activity and the adverse employment action. Indeed, Fokin chastised plaintiff for complaining to the human resources department. (Plaintiff's Depo., 61:18-62:3; Cervantes Decl., par. 26.)

Defendant fails to shift the burden on the failure to prevent claim since plaintiff may be able to establish the underlying claims. (See Carter v. Cal. Dept. of Veterans Affairs (2006) 38 Cal.4th 914, 925, fn. 4 ["courts have required a finding of actual discrimination or harassment under FEHA before a plaintiff may prevail under section 12940, subdivision (k)"]; Trujillo v. N. County Transit Dist. (1998) 63 Cal.App.4th 280, 289.)

There is a triable issue of material fact whether defendant reasonably accommodated plaintiff's alleged disability and whether it interfered with/retaliated against plaintiff for taking CFRA leave. Defendant transferred plaintiff's duties after she went on medical leave and gave her "grunt" work. Defendant also mistreated plaintiff for taking leave. (Cervantes Decl., pars. 17, 18, 20, 22, 23-25.)

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard Ulmer, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/RBU)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Apr-15-2021 Set For Trial On Feb-07-22 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

DEMURRER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Mar-09-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances (via ZOOM videoconference):
Atty. Shannon Seibert, Esq. of Seibert Bautista Montoya, 510.679.1981, appearing for the Plaintiff;
Atty. Lisa B. Berkowitz, Esq., Office of the City Attorney, 415.554.3825, appearing for Defendants Cheryl Sperry and Tania Fokin.

DEFENDANTS CHERYL SPERRY AND TANIA FOKIN'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT.

Matter called, ARGUMENT is held.

At the conclusion of argument, the tentative ruling is adopted as follows: Defendants Fokin and Sperry's demurrer to plaintiff's first amended complaint is sustained without leave to amend. The demurrer to the third cause of action against defendant Fokin for retaliation is sustained because, as the California Supreme Court held squarely in Jones v. Torrey Pines Lodge Partnership (2008) 42 Cal.4th 1158, supervisors may not be held personally liable for retaliation under the FEHA. (Id. at 1173 ["we conclude that the employer is liable for retaliation under section 12940, subdivision (h), but nonemployer individuals are not personally liable for their role in that retaliation"]; see also Lopez v. Routt (2017) 17 Cal.App.5th 1006, 1016-1017 ["FEHA imposes personal liability on individual employees for harassment, but does not impose personal liability on individual employees for employment discrimination or retaliation" (citations omitted)].)

The demurrer to the eighth cause of action against defendant Sperry is sustained without leave to amend, leaving no causes of action against Sperry in this action. The California Family Rights Act (CFRA) covers employers who directly employ five or more persons to perform services for a salary or wage and the state, and any political or civil subdivision of the state and cities. (Gov. Code., sec. 12945.2(b)(3).) Unlike the FMLA, there is no individual liability for retaliation for taking leave under the CFRA. (See Nazir v. United Airlines, Inc. (2009) 178 Cal.App.4th 243, 287 [affirming summary adjudication in favor of supervisor of CFRA retaliation claim].) Thus, a cause of action for retaliation and protection from interference with CFRA rights must be against a covered employee as defined by the CFRA, not an individual supervisor. (2 Cal. Code Regs., sec. 11094.)

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, Email: maria.torreano@gmail.com. (302/EPS)
